If a solid shaft (diameter 20 cm, length 400 cm, N = 0.8 x 105 N/mm2) when subjected to a twisting moment, produces maximum shear stress of 50 N/mm2, the angle of twist in radians, is
	Vishal Patil, 2015, Explain it?
	Rohit Kumar, 2016, By Torsion Equation: T/Ip = Fs/ymax = G$/L.Where T = Torsion Ip = Polar moment of inertia  Ymax = Max distance from nuetral axisG = MODULUS of rigidity$ = Angle of twist in Radian Fs = Max shear force.Hence Fs/Ymax = G$/L So 50/100 = 0.8 * 100000/4000 (All diamension in  MM) = 0.0025 Answer.
	Pinki, 2017, According to me, the Right answer is 0.025.
	Manoj Paridwal, 2017, Yes, @Pinki.The right answer is .025.
	Asay, 2017, By TORSION EQUATION.T/I=Fs/Ymax=G$/L.====> Fs/Ymax=G$/L.====> $=Fs.L/G.Ymax ----> (1).Here,Fs=Maximum Shear Stress=50 N/mm2.And,Ymax=D/2=20/2=10cm=100mmAnd,G=0.8 x 105 N/mm2.$=Twisting Moment=? (Required)L=400cm=4000mm.Now , Putting the Values in eqn (1).$=50*4000/[0.8*100000*(100)].$=0.025.
	Dheeraj, 2020, The right answer is 0.025.

For permissible shear stress fs, the torque transmitted by a thin tube of mean diameter D and wall thickness t, is
	Skm, 2018, How? Explain.

	Sri, 2020, @Aamir thickness is not in the formula.
	Jiban Regmi, 2021, T = Fs*J/R.where, J = π*(Do^4-Di^4)/32.                 =π/32*(Do^2+Di^2)(Do+Di)(Do-Di).                  =  " * "* 2D *2t  (where D=(Do+Di)/2, t=(Do-Di)/2).                   =π*D*t/8 *[(Do-Di)^2+2*Do*Di].                    =π*D*t/8*[ 4t^2 + 2D^2].As the first term has a higher power of t , that can be neglected so;=π*D^3*t/4.Now Torque T=fs*J/R.=π*D^2*t*fs/2.



A compound bar consists of two bars of equal length. Steel bar cross-section is 3500 mm2 and that of brass bar is 3000 mm2. These are subjected to a compressive load 100,000 N. If Eb = 0.2 MN/mm2 and Eb = 0.1 MN/mm2, the stresses developed are:
	Rosnee, 2016, Please explain the calculation.
	Sanjuu, 2016, Explain in details.
	Suman Chapagain, 2016, The assumption made here is the formation made by two bars are equal.
	Suman Chapagain, 2016, Deformation of steel = deformation of brass.Ps.Ls/As.Es = Pb.Lb/Ab.Eb.Here Ls and Lb cancels as lengths are equal. You get from here is Ps =7Pb/3.P = Ps + Pb.Substitution here you get, Ps = 70,000 and Pb = 30,000.Then stress = force/area.You will get the answer.
	Chhaya, 2017, Thanks @Suman.
	Tanvee, 2017, Not getting it. Please explain.
	Ankit Gautam, 2018, Please explain it clearly.
	Monika Verma, 2018, Please anyone explain this.
	Arun, 2018, Stress = PL/AE.6s = Ps L/As Es      6b=Pb L/Ab Eb.Length of both bars is same hence L will be cancelled,By putting values of the respective element.6s= 20N/mm2  6b= 10 N/mm2.
	Imtiaz Hakim, 2020, As it is a compound bar so the strain produce will be the same in both.Strain  PsL/AsEs is equal to  PbL/ AbEb.As length is the same it will be canceled out.So Ps/3500*.2    = Pb/3000*.1.Ps/700 = Pb/300..427Ps = Pb.P= pb + ps.P= .427ps + ps= 1.427ps.100000= 1.427ps.Ps = 70000.100000-70000=Pb.30000=pb.Now stress in steel brass= 70000÷3500= 20N/mm2.Stress in bronze =30000÷3000= 10N/mm2.
	Imtiaz Hakim, 2020, As it is a compound bar strain produced will be the same in both bars...PsL/AsEs   = PbL/AbL.L will be cancelled out.So Ps/3500*2   = Pb/3000*.1  ,     Ps/700  = Pb/300.Or Pb  = 0.428ps. Now P = PB +Ps  = 0.428ps +ps = 1.428Ps.100000=1.428Ps or Ps= 70000N    and Pb = 30000N.Stress = load/ area. stress in steel = 70000/3500 =20N.Stress in brass = 30000/3000 =10N.

A simply supported uniform rectangular bar breadth b, depth d and length L, carries an isolated load W at its mid-span. The same bar experiences an extension e under same tensile load. The ratio of the maximum deflection to the elongation, is
	Ankish Pande, 2016, Can anyone explain it? Please.
	Vipin, 2016, Elongation= WL/AE.Deflection= WL3/EI.(WL/b * d * E)/( WL3/E * b * d3/12) = (L/2d)2.
	Amit, 2016, Deflection = WL3/48EI (correction).
	Sumit, 2017, Elongaton is wl/2ae.
	Himanshu, 2017, WL3/48EI = deflection (I= BD3/12).WL/AE = elongation (A=BD).
	Tina, 2018, Yes, Correct @Himanshu.
	Asraful Adalder, 2018, Elongation= WL/AE.Deflection= WL3/48EI.(WL/b * d * E)/( WL^3/48E * b * d3/12) = (L/2d)2.
	Steve, 2018, The maximum deflection for a simply supported beam is WL^3/48EI.Strain, e =stress/EStress=F/A.e= F/AE,but F=WL;  e= WL/AE.The ratio = Max. deflection/Elongation=(WL^3/48EI)/(WL/AE).But A=b.d; I=bd^3/12.Therefore we have our ratio as= (WL^3/48E*(b * d^3/12))/(WL/b * d * E)= L^2/4d^2= (L/2d)^2.
	Anu, 2020, Perfect explanation, Thanks you @Steve.

	Chhaya, 2017, Slope = wl3/24EI.Moment = wl2/8.Stress =  M/Z.z = bd2/6.I = bd3/12.Slope/stress.You got depth and it is 250.

The yield moment of a cross section is defined as the moment that will just produce the yield stress in
	Ashish, 2018, How? Please tell me.

	Suds, 2020, Yield moment: BM at which the first part of the section reaches yield stress.Plastic moment: BM at which the entire cross section reaches yield stress.So, always, PM>YM.



If a concrete column 200 x 200 mm in cross-section is reinforced with four steel bars of 1200 mm2 total cross-sectional area. Calculate the safe load for the column if permissible stress in concrete is 5 N/mm2 and Es is 15 Ec
	Nazeer, 2016, How is it done?
	Bapan Halder, 2016, P = 5 * {(40000 - 1200) + (1200 * 15)}.
	Mrinal Kanti Biswas, 2016, P = 5*(200*200-1200) + (1200*15) = 212000 NSo the answer is E.
	Abhishek.Kumar, 2016, Sir. Plese tell me the correct answer with explanation.
	Nishant Jain, 2016, Ans is C.Find  permissible stress[$] in steel first as:Permissible stress in steel/permissible stress in concrete = Es/Ec.Permissible stress in steel = 5 * 15Ec/Ec = 75.Now solve:P = 5 * (200 * 200 - 1200) + (1200 * 75) = 284000N = 284.
	Shiva, 2017, Explain this with formula.
	Chulbul, 2018, As the elongations are equal, use the formula.Strain= stress/Young's modulus.
	Maniesh, 2018, Stress= load/ area,Stress in steel = load / area of steel and likewise same for concrete,Es= permissible stress in steel/ strain in steel and likewise same for concrete,strain in concrete and steel are same = permissible stress in steel/ Es=  permissible stress in concrete/ Ec.Es= 15Ec.=permissible stress in steel= permissible stress in concrete/ Ec xEs =( 5 /Ec)x 15Ec= 75Mpa for steel.Load = permissible Stress in steel x area of steel +permissible stress in concrete x area of concrete.= 75 x 1200+ 5 x (200X200 -1200)= 284kN.
	Kisan Prasad Gaire, 2019, Excellent explanation, Thanks @Maniesh.
	Abhishek, 2019, Thank you for explaining.
	Raju, 2019, Thank you @Maniesh.
	Roy, 2019, So,modular ratio is m=Es/Ec=15. Stress in steel, t=mc=15*5 = 75mpa.
	Govind Singh, 2019, Thank you, Nishant Jain, for giving an answer with placid explanation.
	Akshat Kumar, 2020, As=1200.Ac=38800. 6c=5.Es=Ec.We know that,dL=PL/AE=6L/E.Here, dLs=dLc.      6sLs/Es=6cLc/Ec.      Ls=Lc.6s/Es=6c/Ec.6s/15Ec=5/Ec.6s=5/Ec*15Ec.6s=75.Now,P=6sAs+6cAc.P=75*1200+5*38800.P=284000N.
	Nuuui, 2021, As = 1200.Ac = 38800. 6c=5.Es = Ec.We know that,dL = PL/AE = 6L/E.Here,dLs = dLc.6sLs/Es = 6cLc/Ec. Ls = Lc.6s/Es = 6c/Ec.6s/15Ec = 5/Ec.6s = 5/Ec * 15Ec.6s = 75.Now,P = 6sAs + 6cAc.P = 75 * 1200 + 5 * 38800.P = 284000N.

Maximum shear stress theory for the failure of a material at the elastic limit, is known
	Jeet, 2020, Different Theories of Failure : These are five different theories of failures which are generally used.(a) Maximum Principal stress theory (due to Rankine).(b) Maximum shear stress theory (Guest - Tresca).(c) Maximum Principal strain (Saint - Venant) Theory.(d) Total strain energy per unit volume (Haigh) Theory.(e) Shear strain energy per unit volume Theory (Von Mises & Hencky).
	Krishna Kunal, 2020, Thank you @Jeet.

The force in AC of the truss shown in the given figure, is 
	Khushbu, 2020, Please, anyone can explain it?
	Khushbu, 2020, Please discuss in detail.
	Anirban Roy, 2020, There is only one horizontal force 4T acting at the top. This shall pass undiminished to the bottom as it is a pin joint frame. So horizontal reactions at A & B should be 4T each.Respectively, But, notice that B is roller end so horizontal reaction at B=0 and free to move towards right under the action of the force. While horizontal reaction at A remains 4T towards LHS. Thus, while at A it remains fixed, but at B it moves towards the right. Thus creating an extension force on the member AB. So the member AB is under 4T tension.

	Sedfg, 2018, Because taking moment about the only one unknown force is rest so we solve it easily.

Maximum principal stress theory for the failure of a material at elastic point, is known
	Jeet, 2020, Different Theories of Failure : These are five different theories of failures which are generally used.(a) Maximum Principal stress theory (due to Rankine).(b) Maximum shear stress theory (Guest - Tresca).(c) Maximum Principal strain (Saint - Venant) Theory.(d) Total strain energy per unit volume (Haigh) Theory.(e) Shear strain energy per unit volume Theory (Von Mises & Hencky).
	Rashd, 2020, Thank you @Jeet.

A rectangular bar of width b and height h is being used as a cantilever. The loading is in a plane parallel to the side b. The section modulus is
	Mukesh Yadav, 2013, Section modulus = I/Y(max).Then I = (b^3*h)/12.And y = b/2.Then z = (b^2*h)/6.
	Mahesh, 2013, Section modulus = I/y(max).Here the load is parallel to the width b so, I = (b^3*h)/12.And y = b/2.Then z = (b^2*h)/6.
	C.Mallireddy, 2013, Section modulus = I/Y (this is max).Moment of inertia (I) = (d*b^3)/12.                         = (h*b^3)/12.In this problem width = b.Depth d = h.And y = b/2.:-  Z= ( (h*b^3)/12)/(b/2).     Z = (h*b^2)/6.
	Prem Kumar Meena, 2013, Z = I/Y(max.)  I = bd^3/12 ; y(max.) = d/2  load is parallel to side b then b=h and d=b ; Z = hb^3/12/b/2 ; Z = hb^2/6.
	Deepak Singh, 2014, We know,Z = I/Y  [where, y=y(max)].But, I = db^3/12. y = b/2.Now, Z = 2db^3/12d,  = db^2/6  [A/C to question d=h],Z = hb^2/6.
	Raj, 2014, Can somebody explain me "plane parallel to side b"?
	Josh, 2015, Its like load is normal to height h which means parallel to b.
	Ithi, 2015, (hb^3)% (0.5b) = (hb^2%6).

Distance from n.a. to extreme stressed fibre is b/2.

U2 = 0.5xMxtheta.= 0.5x[(fxI)/y]x[(Mxl)/(ExI)].(from simple bending equation & moment area method resp.)Solve it U2 = {(f^2)xvol}/(6xE).i.e U2 = 0.3XU1.:) :) :) :).

The ratio of strengths of solid to hollow shafts, both having outside diameter D and hollow having inside diameter D/2, in torsion, is
	Maggie, 2013, The strength of a solid shaft in torsion is given by,T(solid shaft) = (pi/16)*(max stres)*(D^3).T(hollow shaft) = (pi/16)*(max stres)*(D^4-d^4)/D.On solving we get 15/16.
	Avinash, 2013, max stress = Tymax/Ip; where Ip = polar moment of inertia.ymax = D/2.max stress(solid) = 16T/piD^3.max stress(hollow) = 32T(D/2)/pi(D^4-d^4).Replacing d = D/2.The ratio we get is 15/16.
	Pradhyumna, 2013, Polar modulus is a measure of strength of a shaft in a torsion.So, simply calculate the ratios of polar modulus of solid to hollow shafts.Polar modulus = Polar moment of inertia/Radius of shaft.
	Pawan, 2014, But ratio obtained 16/15 how?
	Murli Meena, 2014, Dia of solid shaft = D.DIA OF HOLLOW SHAFT = d/2.The ratio of solid shaft = 16.The ratio of hollow shaft = 15.Total tension in shafts = 15/16.
	Anil, 2014, The maximum stress due to torsion in solid shaft is 16T/d3.The maximum stress due to torsion in solid shaft is 16x16T/15d3.Therefore ratio maximum stress of solid shaft to hollow shaft is simply: 15/16.Torsional Equation:T/J = s/R = G0/l.
	Virendra, 2014, Ratio will be 16/15.Simply if equal outer diameter then solid shaft will be more strong. But if material are same then hollow shaft will be more strong.
	Raj, 2014, As @Pradhyumna said earlier that polar moment of inertia(J) decide the strength of the member.J for solid section = D^4 and for Hallow section = inner D^4-outer D^4. i.e D^4-(D/2)^4 = 15/16D^4. So the ratio is 15/16.
	Bharat, 2015, Zp(solid)/Zp(hollow) = (pi*D^3/16)/(pi*(D^4-(D/2)^4)/16D) = 16/15.
	Parna Sengupta, 2015, For a hollow shaft whose external diameter is d and internal diameter is d/2, then what will be the polar modulus?
	Umesh, 2015, Shaft is said to be more strong if it resist moment and tmax is not the parameter to be used for it. Tmax is used to compared material property and here we are calculating section strength, so maximum moment of resistant should be compared and answer for it will be 16/15.
	Ajeet, 2015, What is diameter of sun and internal and external heat?
	Amit Pandit, 2015, For outer diameter (odd) moment of inertia = d^4.Inner diameter, moment of inertia = (d/2)^4 = (d^4)/16.For hollow shaft = d^4-(d^4)/16.= (15/16)d^4.So, the ratio of hollow shaft is 15/16.
	Raju Katara, 2016, A hollow steel shaft of 300 mm external diameter and 200 mm internal diameter has to be replaced by a solid steel shaft of 300 mm external diameter and 200 mm internal diameter has to be replaced by a solid steel shaft of?Give me the answer.
	Dwarika Prasad Sahoo, 2016, If Solid and Hollow shaft are of the same material and same weight, then Hollow shaft has more strength. But when outer diameter is same (weight will not be same) then Solid shaft has more strength, so here ratio of the strength of solid and Hollow shaft should be more than 1 as outer diameter same.
	Shatakshi, 2017, It's true, ultimately the ratio of Tmax (Solid) / Tmax (Hollow) = D^4/(D^4-d^4).Let D=20 mm & d=10mm, then D^4=160000 mm^4 & d^4=10000 mm^4.Therefore, D^4/(D^4-d^4) = 16/15.
	Raghavendra, 2017, Yeah, the answer must be 16/15.
	Krishnavn, 2017, Here, the answer must be 16/15.
	Bhagchand Golhani, 2017, Zp= π/32(d^4).So,      Zp(s)/Zp(h)=[π/32d^4]/[π/32*(15d^4)/16],                  =16/15.
	Maharaj, 2017, @Bhagchand.Yes, you are right.The answer is 16/15.
	Ravikumar, 2017, The solid section  max shear stress is 16T/pi D3.              Hollow section is 16T/piD^3*[1-[D^4/d^4]].       The ratio of these to is 15/16 once remembering d=D/2.
	Akshay, 2017, I also get Answer is 16/15.
	Salman, 2017, Shear stress= 16T/πD3(solid).Shear stress=16T/πD3(1-d4/D4)                      =16T/π(D4-d4)/D                      =16T/π/(15D4/16)/D                      =16*16T/(π*15*D3)Ratio=16T/πD3:16*16T/15πD3.          = 15/16.
	Gangadhar, 2018, Simply says (1-(1^4&div;2&div;4)=15&div;16.

The weakest section of a diamond riveting, is the section which passes through
	Neelam, 2013, Weakest section in riveting at which maximum stress are developed and in a diamond riveting, it is first row.
	Nabin Neupane, 2013, Could you please tell me how is the diamond riveting?
	Ram, 2015, Section in first row is weakest because shear strength in first row is of only. 1 Rivet and in second row shear strength is of 2 rivets and further increasing.
	John, 2015, Anyone have link to explain this diamond rivet entering first row? Picture or video.
	Akash, 2015, Let Strength of each bolt = 100KN.I. Critical state of 1st sec= (600/100) = 6.--->600KN.II. Critical state of 2nd sec = (600-100/100+100) = 2.5.(since 100KN load is taken by 1st bolt, net load transferred to second section = 600-100).III. Critical state of 3rd sec = (500-200/100+100+100) = 1.Critical condition of a bolt = (load applied/strength of bolt.Hence first section is critical section (6>2. 5>1).
	Amitjot Kamboj, 2016, All the explanations are not upto the mark.
	Parth Dodiya, 2017, There is border of the diamond rivet that mean it's the first row at that point maxi. Shear stress occurs, so it's the weakest point of the diamond rivet is the first row.
	Sanjay, 2017, Can Anyone explain critical section for diamond riveting?
	Roy, 2018, Option D is the correct answer.
	Akv, 2018, Suppose there is 6 bolt.1 in the first row, 2 in the second row and 3 in the last row.For the first row, strength is due to the net sectional area and one rivet.For second row strength is due to the net sectional area and 3 rivet 1 from the first row and 2 from other rows.For third row, strength is due to the net sectional area and 6 rivet.But net sectional area1> 2 >3.
	Rahul Kumar Singh, 2018, Suppose there is 6 bolt.1 in the first row, 2 in the second row and 3 in the last row.For the first row, strength is due to the net sectional area and one rivet.For second row strength is due to the net sectional area and 3 rivet 1 from the first row and 2 from other rows.For the third row, strength is due to the net sectional area and 6 rivet.But net sectional area1> 2 >3.

	Raghavendra, 2013, Meaning of contraflexure is contra opposite and flexure means bending totally it means zero bending moment or change in sign of bending moment.

The ratio of elongations of a conical bar due to its own weight and that of a prismatic bar of the same length, is
	Neelam Shekhawat, 2013, Elongation of a conical bar due to its own weight is = (lemma) *L^2 divided by 6AE.Elongation of a prismatic bar is = (lemda)*L^2 divided by 2AE.
	Munish, 2016, Deflection due to self weight = wl/2AE.Deflection of 2nd beam = Wl/2AE + WL/AE.Equate and Ratio is 1:3.
	Munish, 2016, The solution for both beams when prismatic one carry additional load w with weight w.
	Mujru, 2016, Γ * length. Squre/2 E.Γ = unit weight of material.E = young modulus.But here bar said to be conical so the 1/3 multiple the 1st equation.So the final equation is;.Γ * length squre/6E.So the answer is 1/3.
	Deepal Maheshwari, 2016, Elongation = PL/AE.Now the weight of a bar of the conical shape is 1/3φr2h assuming both bars of the same material so neglecting density weight = volume.And, the weight of the prismatic bar is φr2h.So the weight of the conical bar is 1/3 to the prismatic bar.Elongation in conical bar = PL/3AE.Elongation in a prismatic bar = PL/AE .Here, Ratio will be 1:3.
	Baloch, 2016, Elongation of the prismatic bar due to its own weight = w l ^ 2 /2E .Elongation of the conical bar due to its own weight = w l ^ 2 /6E.wl^2/2E  &div; wl^2 / 6E,wl^2/2E  x  6E / wl^2,(wl^2 and E canceled)2/6 = 1/3.
	Durgesh, 2017, If length and weight of both prismatic and conical same. Then the ratio of their elongation due to their own weight will be?Please give me the answer.
	Sourabh, 2017, @Durgesh.Weight of conical bar is 1/3πrrh unit.Weight of prismatic bar πrrh.
	Navjeet Singh Nit Patna, 2017, Because  for prismatic bar =YL^2/2E. and  for conical bar     =YL^2/6E.So that ratio =YL^2/6E*2E/YL^2=1/3.
	Azzu, 2018, For conical bar = γ l^2/(6E).Prismatic bar=γ l^2/(2E),Ratio of above values =1/3.
	Pritish, 2018, (WL^2/6E)/(WL^2/2E) = 1/3.
	Vicky, 2018, Isn't there any difference between Y (γ) and W (weight)?
	Monika, 2018, Y is weight density. That is w = Y * volume.
	Lakhan Bhavnani, 2018, WL^2/6AE for the conical bar.AndWL^2/2AE for the prismatic bar.Then ratio of the conical bar to prismatic bar is;(WL^2/6AE)/(WL^2/2AE) = 1/3(ANS).
	Vetan, 2019, Elongation of a cylinder rod of diameter D,Length L and wt density w.Wl2/2E.Elongation of a conical rod of diameter D, at support with Length L and wt density w.Wl2/6E.Its ratio is 1/3.
	Parth, 2020, Simple solution: The Volume of Cone = 1/3. (π). r^2. h.Volume of prismatic bar(always cylinder) = (π). r^2. h.Take the ratio cone/cylinder: 1/3.



A three-hinged arch is said to be :
	Teja, 2014, Why it was statically determinate structure?
	Vicky, 2014, Why option A is right?
	Mayank Chetiwal, 2014, since support reactions are 4 and equilibrium equations were 3 and because of a hinge 1 additional equilibrium will be added So,D = R-S.R = 4.S = 3+1 = 4.D = 4-4 = 0.Statically determinate.

The deflection due to couple M at the free end of a cantilever length L is
	Mounika Reddy, 2013, EI(d^2y/dx^2) = M.(d^2y/dx^2) = M/EI.dy/dx = (M/EI)*X+C1.y = (M/2EI)X^2+C1X+C2.AT X=0, Y=0 SO C2=0.AT X=0, DY/DX=0 MEANS SLOPE ZERO, SO C1=0.Y = (M/2EI)X^2 AT X = L.Y = (M/2EI)L^2.
	L S, 2015, IT is ML^3/3EI = Deflection.ML^2/2EI is Slope.
	K.V.U Karthik, 2015, We have already learnt this in strength of materials subject in second year that too its a basic one simple for cantilever beam. Slope is ML^2/2EI.Thank you.
	Karan, 2015, Question is not talking about load, it is asking about moment. That's why option C is correct.
	Ved, 2015, By conjugate beam method finding BM for conjugate Beam is equivalent to deflection of desired beam so C is answer.
	Lucky, 2017, The moment equation is m.Integrate it w.r.t length, we will get slope eq ML.Again integrate it will get we will get deflection equation ML^2.So ml^2/EI.
	Gaurav, 2018, Option A is the correct answer.
	Uma Shankar, 2019, Option C is the right answer.
	Vetan, 2019, Slope is ML/EI.Deflection is ML2/2EI.



The shape of the bending moment diagram over the length of a beam, having no external load, is always
	Bibek Gupta, 2013, If the beam having no external load then self weight will be act as udl throughout the entire span, for that reason the BM diagram will be parabola.
	Neelam Shekhawat, 2013, If the beam having no external load then it would not be bend. So shape always will be liner. And self weight will be act as udl but it does not change the shape because beam carries its weight without any bending.
	Vinay, 2014, Yes I agree it is parabola since the dead load of the beam is UDL.
	Diwakar, 2015, Yes I think parabola is correct answer.
	Harish, 2015, In a theory it states that beam linear before application of load (self weight is neglected) so linear is correct.
	Devi, 2015, I think Parabola answer is correct.

	Kyu, 2014, Forces along y-axis are along a straight line, right? then how is it different from forces not along the straight line?

	Subinsekhar, 2015, If each force is zero, then there is no force at all. Answer B is wrong.

A closely coiled helical spring of radius R, contains n turns and is subjected to an axial load W. If the radius of the coil wire is r and modulus of rigidity of the coil material is C, the stress developed in the helical spring is
	Piyush Singh, 2014, Maximum stress developed by the spring :Tmax = (16PR)/(nD^3).D = 2r.P = W.Tmax = (2WR)/(nr^3).
	Mahesh, 2016, Twisting moment T = W * R and torsional stress applicable then T/J= ts/r.
	Kjick, 2016, Not understand, Please explain in detail.
	Praveen Pawar, 2017, Maximum stress developed by the spring :Tmax = (16T)/(nD^3).D = 2r, T = WR.Tmax = (16WR)/(n8r^3).          = 2WR/nr^3.

	Pavani, 2020, T=16wR/πd^3 is the formula for the stress developed in the helical spring. put d=r you will get the answer.

A simply supported beam of span L carries a concentrated load W at its mid-span. The maximum bending moment M is
	Vinay, 2013, Support reactions = w/2,Max bending moment occurs at point of loading acting for symmetrical  loading,= w/2*l/2= wl/4
	Devendranaik, 2016, IN SIMPLE SUPPORTED BEAM of length L.Then the reactions at each ends = W/2.Then we calculate the half if the length of the beam = L/2 (L=length of the beam is L).HERE SHEAR FORCE  = WL/2 (from the left end).BENDING MOMENT = W/2 * L/2 = WL/4.
	Asay, 2017, Since in simply supported beams, the bending moment is zero at the support ends, So it should be maximum at the centre (i-e at point of loading).But,Reactions at the supports will be W/2 each.So, Bending Moment (max) at the centre = (W/2)(L/2)=WL/4 (Option B).
	Shasha, 2018, What is the bending moment value for fixed support beam of span L carries the concentrated load at mid span?



The ratio of the maximum deflections of a beam simply supported at its ends with an isolated central load and that of with a uniformly distributed load over its entire length, is
	Mayank Jain, 2013, Maximum deflections of a beam simply supported at its ends with an isolated central load = (WL^3)/(48EI)Maximum deflections of a beam simply supported at its ends with uniformly distributed load= (5wL^4)/(384EI)     {W=wL}So [(WL^3)/(48EI)] / [(5wL^4)/(384EI)] = 24/15.
	Souryadeep Chaki, 2014, Maximum deflections of a beam simply supported at its ends with an isolated central load = (WL^3)/(48EI).Maximum deflections of a beam simply supported at its ends with uniformly distributed load= (5wL^4)/(384EI) {W=wL}.So [(WL^3)/(48EI)] / [(5wL^4)/(384EI)] = 8/5.
	Bhakti Deshmukh, 2015, @Bhakti deshmukh:1) Max. Deflection of ssb subjected to central load = WL^3/48 EI.2) Max. Deflection of beam subjected to udl = (5wL^4)/(384EI).Hence ratio of these two is 8/5.
	Mayuri, 2015, Ratio is 8/5, hence multiplying by 3 we will get 24/15 which is given in options.
	Manoranjan, 2016, I am not getting this, Please, Properly discuss this question.
	Sanjeev Kumar, 2017, Answer is 8/5. Why we need to multiply the factor 3?
	Vicky, 2017, Question is asking max deflection at ends and these formulas are for max deflection at centre.Clear it please.
	Jyothi, 2017, Max Deflection where occurs in the middle only. At supports, it is zero so 8/5 is the answer. But why 24/15?
	Nikhil, 2017, 24/15 is equal to 8/5 so 24/15 is correct answer.
	Indu, 2017, How 24/15 is correct, could you explain me please clearly?
	Shubham, 2017, The answer is 8L/5.
	Kallan, 2017, 8/5 = 1.6,24/15 = 1.6,Both are same.
	Ezaaz Ahmed, 2017, Maximum Deflection at the mid-span of a SSB with an isolated central load is WL3/48EI--> iMaximum deflection at the mid-span of a SSB with UDL through the entire span is 5WL3/384EI--> ii.i/ii=8/5.So the correct answer is 8/5.
	Prasanth, 2017, But some books said 5/8, they took udl deflection divided by point load deflection.
	Deepak Singh Bisht, 2018, 8/5l is the right answer.
	Mekonnen S., 2018, 8/5 is the right answer. Therefore, 8/5 * 3/3 = 24/15 is correct.
	Shankar Gubbala, 2019, The isolated load is nothing but point load. So,For simply supported beam deflection is wl^3/48EI.For UDL 5WL^4/384EI,So the ratio of these two is 24/15,Again 24 and 15 cancel in 3 table,Finally we get 8/5.
	Rajeev Kumar, 2019, Yes, it is 8÷5. I too agree.
	Rahul, 2019, 8/5 * 3 = 24/15.
	Thiru, 2019, 8/5 original answer but this answer multiply with 3/3 then get options answer. (8/5)*(3/3) = 8*3/5*3 = 24/15.

A triangular section having base b, height h, is placed with its base horizontal. If the shear stress at a depth y from top is q, the maximum shear stress is
	Manish Kumar, 2014, Max shear stress = 1.5*s/(area) and area is 0.5*bh^2.
	Logu, 2014, What is S?
	Vyshu, 2015, Here S is force.

	Dhrubajyoti, 2015, Please show me the whole calculation.
	Kiran, 2015, Area only 0.5*bh. Not 0.5*bh^2.

	Yps, 2016, The right answer should be 1.5s/bh.
	Harish, 2016, Yes, the right answer is 1.5 s/area for triangle.
	Prabhu, 2017, The formula is 3/2(F/A).F=S & A = 1/2(bh).

	Sheela, 2017, Thanks @Krishna.
	Ziya, 2017, S = Shear force.Maximum shear stress = 3S/bh.

	Khemraj Bind, 2018, S = shear stress.Area of triangle=0.5 * bh.Maximum shear stress of triangle = Average shear stress of triangle 3/2.     = (3/2) *  S/A,     = (1.5 S) /0.5 bh,     = 3S/bh.  The correct answer is option A.

The slenderness ratio of a vertical column of a square cross-section of 2.5 cm sides and 300 cm length, is
	Kaushal, 2013, According to me, Slenderness ratio is kl/b. where k is length factor, l is the length of column and b is the width.Let us suppose k = 1 (pin jointed both sides).Slenderness ratio = 1*300/2.5 = 120.How could answer be 416?
	Raju, 2013, kl = 300.r = (i%a)0.5.Slender ratio = kl%r.i = moment of inertia = b*d3%12.a = cross section area = 2.2*2.5.
	Pradhyumna, 2013, Dear @Kaushal, clear the definition first.Slenderness ratio = Unsupported length of column/minimum radius of gyration of cross-section.Hence, SR = 300/rad. of gyration. = 300/ sq.root of ((2.5^4/(12*2.5^2))). = 415.692. = 416.
	Pawan Kumar, 2014, You are right @Pradhyumna.Slenderness ratio = l/k = 300/0.7217= 415.692=416.K = radius of gyration = root under(I/A) = under root 0.52.I/A = (moment of inertia/area) = 2.5*2.5*2.5*2.5/12)/(2.5*2.5)=0.52.
	Piyush Singh, 2014, Slenderness Ratio (lemma ) = L(eff) / R(min).Rmin = under root of ( Imin / area ).Imin  = (b*d^3) / 12.Area  = (b*d).(b=d).Imin / Area = b^2 / 12.Rmin = under root of (b*b / 12).Rmin = 0.2886 b^ 2.Slender ness Ratio = (Leff / R min).                   = (300 / ( 0.886*2.5).                   = 415.96 = 416.
	Deepak Goel, 2014, Its value is wrong because slenderness ratio is l/r means 300/2.5 = 120.
	Xyz, 2014, In case of Strength of materials, slenderness ratio is eff length/radius of gyration, while in case of RCC it is eff length/least lateral dimension.
	Anil Baral, 2014, r = sqrt (I/A).A = 2.5*2.5.I = 2.5^/4.There fore, r = 2.5*sqrt (1/12).r = 300/2.5*sqrt (1/12).= 415.69 approx=416.

If n is the ratio of internal and external diameters of a hollow shaft, the ratio of the weight of the hollow shaft and that of solid shaft of same strength, will be
	M.Raja Reddy(Svkp Cumbum), 2016, Anyone give explanation for the answer.
	Dalisha, 2016, Given, d/D = n and stress ratio D^4 - d^4/D^4 = Th/Ts.Now, d = nD, D = d/n putin above and get 1 - n^2/(1 - n^4)^2/3.
	Ram Krishna Adhikari, 2016, Now, d = nD, D = d/n.Ratio of the weight = ratio of C/A = (d^2 * (1/n^2-1))/D^2 ...1.Find d^/D^2 as follows:((d/n)^3 - d^3)/D^3 = Th/Ts = 1(d/D)^2 = (1-n^2)/(1-n^4)^(2/3).Substitute in eq 1 you will get the answer.
	Tailam Mahesh, 2017, I can't understand. Can anyone explain clearly?
	Latha, 2017, Please expain clearly.
	Prasenjit, 2018, Explain it please.
	Krishna Sai, 2018, Assume External diameter = d then internal diameter = nd.As both the cases have equal strength so equate both strength which is polar modulus case 1 polar modulus = pi()*(d^4-nd^4)/32/d/2case 2 polar modulus = pi()*(D^3)/16.Equating both find D in terms of n pi()*(d^4-nd^4)/32/d/2 = pi()*(D^3)/16D = d((1-n^4)^1/3.Now weight is directly proportional to Area. area in case 1 = pi()*(d^2-nd^2)/4,Area in case 2 = PI()*(d((1-n^4)^1/3)^2/4,dividing both we get the ratio as (1-n^2)/(1-n^4)^2/3.
	Thakur, 2018, Thanks @Krishna.
	Sheela, 2018, Thanks @Krishna.
	Vikas Kumar, 2018, What is polar modulus?And what is p=, I=?
	Shankar, 2018, The strength of shaft means max. torque or max power transmitted capacity.Here asking their strength equal means weight ratio of the shaft. So weight depends on the size of the shaft. We taking maximum torque equation of finding dia of the shaft. Consider d for outer dia of hollow shaft and nd for inner dia of the shaft. Then formula   = (π) * [ d^4 - (nd)^4] * shear stress /16/d^4.Assume dia of solid shaft  D.Then we  equating torque of solid shaft  = torque of the hollow shaft we get D =  { [d^4 - (nd)^4 ]/ d^4 }The weight depends on the area of the shaft section so,Hollow shaft cross-section area / solid shaft cross-section area. By substituting the value of solid shaft dia in area formula we get the final answer.
	U.S.Lakshmi, 2019, I am going to prepare with this help after second carrying after two years so please explain with notations and clearly.
	Manvitha, 2020, Thank you @Krishna.
	Anvesh, 2020, Thank you @Krishna.



A rectangular beam 20 cm wide is subjected to a maximum shearing force of 10, 000 kg, the corresponding maximum shearing stress being 30 kg/cm2. The depth of the beam is
	Mounika Reddy, 2013, T = SQ/Ib.S=10000, T=30, Q=bh^2/8, I=bh^3/12.Then h = 25cm, orTmax = (3*Vmax)/(2*A).h = 25cm, how will be 15cm.
	Arpita Saxena, 2014, Max. shear stress = 1.5*avg.shear stress.Avg.shear stress = S.F/(B*d).Avg.shear stress = 10000/(20*d).30 = 1.5*10000/(20*d).d = 25cm.
	Abhay, 2016, Maximum shearing stress σ max = 3P/2(bd).where P = Shearing force.30 = 3*10000/(2*20*d) => d = 25 cm.
	Krishna, 2016, Convert the given max to average shear stress and then proceed using,T(max) = 2/3 ( T(avg)).
	Bibek Dey, 2017, Since, maximum shearing stress = 1.5 * average shearing stress.Average shearing stress = V/bd (V=shear force).                                                                                                                                                            30 = 1.5 * 10000(20/d).So, d = 25 cm.

For a given material Young's modulus is 200 GN/m2 and modulus of rigidity is 80 GN/m2. The value of Poisson's ratio is
	Nitin Ingole, 2013, Answer should be 0.25 Poisson's ration = ( E-2G )/2G = (200-2x80)/2*80 = 0.25.
	Rathi, 2013, Relation between young's modulus(E), Rigidity modulus(G) and Poisson's ratio(u).E = 2G(1+u).u = E/2G - 1.  = 200/2*80  - 1.u  = 0.25.
	Manasa Sundaram, 2014, c = mE/2(m+1).1+(1/m) = (2*10^5)/(2*(8*10^4)).1+u = 1.25.u = 0.25.
	Vikas Kumar, 2014, Formula of poisson ratio = (E-2G)/2G.Where E = Young's modulus.            G = Modulus of Rigidity.Now U = (200-2*80)/2*80 = 0.25.
	Mayuri, 2014, E = 2G (1-1/m).200 = 2*80(1-1/m).200 = 160-160/m.40 = 160/m.1/m = 40/160.1/m = 0.25.
	Piyush Singh, 2015, Young modulus = E.Modulus of Rigidity = G.Poisson's Ratio = U.Formula => E = 2G (1-u).Therefore:A) 200 = 2*80 (1-u).B) 1-u = 1.25.C) u = 1.25-1.D) u = 0.25.
	Susmita, 2015, We know that C = mE/2 (m+1).Where C = 80GN/m2.E = 200GN/m2.1/m = Poisson's ratio = 0.25.
	Abhay, 2016, Young's modulus E = 2G(1 + μ) where, G = Modulus of rigidity.=> μ = E/2G - 1.=> 200/(2*80) - 1.= 1.25 - 1.μ = 0.25.
	Krishna, 2016, You are correct @Rathi.E = 2G(1+mu)then u = 0.25.
	Mujru, 2016, The equation related to bulk modulus and modulus of rigidity is;E = 2n(1+u).E = bulk modulus,n= modulus of rigidity, u = possion raito.So,u = E/2n -1.= 200/(80 * 2) - 1.= 0.25.
	Ammulu, 2016, E = 2g(1 + u),200 = 2 * 80(1 + u),200 = 160 + 160u,U = -40 &div; 160,U = 1 &div; 4,U = 0.25.
	Abigail, 2017, I tried d formulae for the  relationship between bulk modulus and modulus of rigidity but it's not giving me the right answer.Can anyone help me?
	Anusha Reddy, 2017, Good explanation @Ammulu.
	Bikash Kabiraj, 2017, Relation between e[young], k[bulk], c[mod of rig] and 1/m[poiss].e = 3k(1-2/m)=2c(1+1/m)=9kc/3k+c,                    e = 200 and c=80.                                                       e = 2c(1+1/m) so 200=2*80(1+1/m) so 1/m=40 /160 =. 25ans.
	Gondelamahesh, 2017, If the modulus of rigidity is 80 kn/mm square and bulk modulus 140 kn/mm square position ratio is what anyone explain it?
	Shruti, 2017, E = 2G( 1+U),200= 2 * 80( 1+U),200/160 = (1+U),5/4-= (1+U),5 = 4(1+U)5=4+4U,4U = 5-4,u= 1/4 = 0.25.
	Chakradhar Padhan, 2017, Relation between e[young], k[bulk], c[mod of rig] and 1/m[poiss].e = 3k(1-2/m)=2c(1+1/m)=9kc/3k+c,e = 200 and c=80.e = 2c(1+1/m).So 200=2*80(1+1/m) so 1/m=40 /160 =. 25.
	Pradip Behera, 2018, Here, E=2G(1+u)=3K(1-2u)=9KG/(3K+G).
	Akshata, 2018, 1st find Youngs Modulus (E) using E= (9KG)/(G+3K) where K=bulk modulus & G= modulus of rigidity then E=2G (1+u) where u= Poisson's ratio.
	Viru Kapoor, 2018, E=2G(1+U).Given E=200.G=80.200=2 * 80(1+u).200/2 * 80=1+u,1.25=1+u,u=1.25 - 1=0.25.

	Maxwell, 2019, Please anyone solve this, If the modulus of rigidity is given as 42MN/mm square and the modulus of elasticity is 200 KN/mm square, what will be an acceptable value for bulk modulus if the material has a cuboid configuration?
	Kowsalya, 2021, E = 200*10^9N/m^2.G = 80*10^9N/m^2.U=?E=2G(1+U).(200 * 10^9)/(2 * 80 * 10^9) = (1+U),100/80 = 1+U,(10/8)-1 = U,(5/4)-1 = U,(5-4)/4 = U.1/4 = U==>U = 0.25.

If the width of a simply supported beam carrying an isolated load at its centre is doubled, the deflection of the beam at the centre is changed by
	Mounika Reddy, 2013, y = (wl^3)/(48*EI) FOR WIDTH b.If it is 2b, I = 2bh^3/12.y' = (wl^3)/(48*E2I).So it is 1/2.
	Bhavsingh, 2015, Deflection = wl^3/48EI.So, from data b = 2b (double).MOI = bd^3/12 = (2bd^3/12).y = (wl^3)/48E (2bd^3)/12).y = 1/2 ((wl^3)/(48EI)).
	Baloch, 2016, Only by using 2nd moment,= bd^3/12 divided by 2bd^3/12.ORbd^3/12 X 12/2bd^3 (after canceling what left behind is 1/2).
	Asif Ali Mundrani, 2017, If width=b,Then, I=bh^3/12.So,(Def)max=PL^3/48EINow,If width=b'=2bThen, I'=b'h^3/12=(2b)h^3/12=2(bh^3/12)I'=2ISo,(Def)'max = PL^3/48EI'= PL^3/48E(2I)= 1/2[PL^3/48EI](Def)'max = 1/2(Def)maxHence Proved That,If the width of a simply supported beam carrying an isolated load at its centre is doubled, the deflection of the beam at the centre is changed by (1/2).
	Amar, 2018, Thanks to all for the explanation.
	Rohan, 2018, Good thanks @Asif Ali Mundrani.
	Govinda, 2019, Thanks to all for explaining the answer.
	Arsalan Wazir, 2019, Deflection has an inverse relation with square of the area of the beam .deflection = 1/(A)2   ie A=bd.So if we increase the width of the beam. Then;Deflection =1/(2bd)2.Deflection= 1/4(bd)2.Deflection will be decreased by 1/4.
	Jinish, 2019, Deflection at centre = wl^3/48EI where I= bd^3/12. Now b = 2b so I= 2bd^3/12 so I=bd^3/6.Now deflection= WL^3/48E*(bd^3/6).                           = WL^3* (6)/  bd^3 * (48).                           = 1/8.Now changed of beam = deflection normal/ deflection due to doubled width.= 1/(1/8)= 8.So the centre is changed by 8 times.

	Kashi, 2013, Maximum bending moment occurs at zero shear force*.d(M)/dx = 0 (for maximum bending moment).on differentiating above equation you get option C.

If a rectangular beam measuring 10 x 18 x 400 cm carries a unformly distributed load such that the bending stress developed is 100 kg/cm2. The intensity of the load per metre length, is
	George Zac, 2014, The correct answer is 270 kg.Bending Stress = f = M/Z (Moment / section Modulus).100 kg/cm2 = (WL^2/8)/(1/6 bd^3)     M = 100/6 x 10 x 18^2.M = 54,000 Kg.cm  - 540 Kg.m.M = WL^2/8= 540.W = 8 * M / L^2.W = 8 * 540 / 4^2.W = 270 Kg.
	John Parker, 2015, Correct option is (D) , 270 Kg.
	Krishna, 2016, F= My/I.M = (WL^2) /8.M = fI/y = wl^2/8.I = bh^3/12.Y = h/2.F and l are known, then we get,W= 270 kg.

	Nikhil, 2013, Where N = The speed in r.p.m.              T = the torque in kgm.Therefore,          P=2*(py)*N*T/4500.Where (py) is 3.141.

A simply supported beam carrying a uniformly distributed load over its whole span, is propped at the centre of the span so that the beam is held to the level of the end supports. The reaction of the prop will be
	Amro, 2016, The beam held to the level of end means deflection equals to zero @ med span.Deflection from distributed load = 5wl^4/384EI (EQ: 1).Deflection from PROP load = PL^3/48EI (EQ: 2).From eq1 & eq2.5wl^4/384EI = PL^3/48EI.P = 5wl/8.

	Mukesh Kumar, 2020, Ans is 3wl/8.As at simply supported beam deflection is zero.Deflection due to distributed load w is wl^4/8EI,Deflection due to support reaction is pl^3/3EI,Both are equal so p=3wl/8.

For a channel section, the shear centre lies at a distance of
	Skm, 2018, How is it? Please explain.
	Ravi Kumar Ranjan, 2020, Distance of shear centre(e).1) For channel section, e=b^2d^2t/4I   ( where t=thickness of channel,b=width of channel,d=depth of channel).2) For semicircular sectione,e=4R/π3) For circular slit e=2R.

	Prateek, 2017, Because its weight is maximum at the top.

If a shaft is simultaneously subjected to a toque T and a bending moment M, the ratio of maximum bending stress and maximum shearing stress is
	Rajendra, 2015, Maximum bending stress = 32M/(pi)D*D*D.Maximum shear stress = 16T/(pi)D*D*D.Ratio of both gives the answer 2M/T.

	Rohit, 2016, Here, dV/v = 3 * (sigma/E * (1 - 2/m)).It is 3 times because cube is subjected to 3 mutually perpendicular stresses.

The section modulus of a rectangular light beam 25 metres long is 12.500 cm3. The beam is simply supported at its ends and carries a longitudinal axial tensile load of 10 tonnes in addition to a point load of 4 tonnes at the centre. The maximum stress in the bottom most fibre at the mid span section, is
	Aspire, 2016, How to solve this?
	Ramachandraraju, 2016, Anybody give explanation about this.
	Akscivilian, 2016, Question is wrong ,there is needed cross-sectional area, not given so we can't find the answer.B.S = M/Z + P/A. M = WL/4.But A is not given.
	Deb, 2017, Please, someone solve it clearly.
	Thakur, 2017, Please explain the details.
	Paul, 2018, How to find out A? Please explain in detail.
	Muthu, 2019, Please explain the answer.
	Pranjul, 2019, Area calculate from bending equation M = f * Z and f = force/area.
	Basanth Babu, 2019, The cross sectional area of the beam is needed to solve this problem.
	Vipin Sainath, 2020, Bending Stress (Sigma) = M/Z x P/A.First, we have to calculate Depth and Breadth ..From span/depth ratio we get DepthSpan/20 = 25m/20 = 1.25m or 125cm so D= 125cm, Then B/D=0.5 to 0.67 from this ratio we get breadth B, B = Dx0.5 = 125X0.5 = 62.5 say B= 60cm, So we got B and D.M/Z = (4000x2500/4) / 12.6 = 2,00,000 kg/cm2.P/A = 10000/7500 =1.33 kg/cm2.Therefore,  Sigma = 200000 kg/cm2 x 1.33 kg/cm2  = 266000 Kg/cm2 or  26.6 Kg/m2finally we got Max stress = 26.6kg/m2.
	Shraddha, 2020, @Vipin Sainath.Why Σ = (M/Z)*(P/A)?
	Kajal, 2020, @Vipin Sainath.Why multiplying p/A?
	Chris, 2021, @Shraddha @Kajal.P/A represents stress.The bending formula:M/I = f/y.Stress f = P/A.Section Modulus Z = I/y.
	Shahid Khan, 2021, Here is the solution:Length= 2.5m=250 cm,Z=12500 cm^3,Z=bh^2/6=12500,Bh^2=75000,H=50cm.B=30 cm.Sigma=m/z + p/A.M=4000*250/4=250000,A=1500 cm2.Sigma =250000/12500 +10000/1500,= 20+6.66,= 26.66 kg/cm2.

The maximum stress intensity due to a suddenly applied load is x-times the stress intensity produced by the load of the same magnitude applied gradually. The value of x is
	Bikash Kabiraj, 2017, We know, that suddenly load is 2 times of gradually load.
	Rushi, 2019, Gradually applied load is given as σ = (F/A)  where, F is the gradually applied load.here, work done is given as (F δL)/2.strain energy stored = (σ2 / 2E) AL.Work done is equal to the strain energy stored.(F δL) / 2 = (σ2 / 2E) ALso, σ = (F/A) --> (1)  Suddenly applied load is given as σ = (2F/A), here work done = (F δL). (F δL) = (σ2 / 2E) AL.Therefore, σ = (2F/A) --> (2) From (1) and (2), it can be concluded that;Hence, suddenly applied load is twice the gradually applied load.

The ratio of the tensile stress developed in the wall of a boiler in the circumferential direction to the tensile stress in the axial direction, is
	Vinod, 2015, Circumferential Tensile stress in a boiler is, sigma = (P.d)/(2t)----1.Axial Tensile stress in a boiler is, sigma = (P.d)/(4t)----2.Now the ratio is = (Equation 1)/(Equation 2).Answer: 2.

A short masonry pillar is 60 cm x 60 cm in cross-section, the core of the pillar is a square whose side is
	Ankit, 2015, Please explain this answer.
	Fogg, 2016, Can anyone explain this?
	Ranjan Patra, 2016, Side = qrt over of sq 10 + sq10 = 14.44.
	Prateek, 2016, @Ranjan Patra.Where the 10 comes from?
	Anonymous, 2016, Side dimension is 60.So, the distance from center within which if load is applied than column does not go under the tension i.e. b/6 = 60/6 = 10, which give the value 10. As per right angle tringle, you get the side of core that is 14.14.
	Fgf, 2016, Can you please explain it step by step?
	Yogesh, 2016, For a rectangular column, kernel core is a rhombus with diagonals B/3 & D/3 resp.In the above Question, dimensions of the core are asked in terms of SIDE. So, to apply Pythagoras theorem, we must consider a quarter of the rhombus, i.e., a triangle which has the sides B/6 & D/6So, the side of the rhombus = hypotenuse of the triangle = sq root (10^2 + 10^2) = sq root(200) = 14.14
	Umesh, 2016, Thanks @Yogesh.
	Neel, 2018, The shape of the core for the square column is square, not a rhombus.so, ans is b/6 i.e. 60/3=20.
	Gopal, 2018, Kern of a square section is also a square so the sides are b/3 & b/3 hence the answer should be 60/3= 20.If it is a rectangle of 60x60 then the above-given approach is correct.
	Anand, 2018, The shape of the core in the square section is square and side value is b/3 & d/3 so the answer is 20 cm.
	Sumit, 2018, Thanks all for explaining.
	Subhankar, 2019, Here, we can use ((B^2 +D^2)^.5)/6 for calculation.
	Ramya, 2019, Thank you @Yogesh.
	Deepankar, 2021, Its a sq.colum as its dimensions are equal,For square column,the shape of kern is also square with diagonal = d/3 = 60/3 = 20cm.Now the Q asks about the side of the core not the diagonal of the core.But we know that Area of square=1/2(diagonal)^2.Also area of sq.=a^2,Solving these two we get a=diagonal/(underroot of 2).So a=20/(under root 2)= 14.14cm.

Now  sum of all vertical forces = 1000 kg,  we get Ay = 750 kg.Now take section of the arch at crown where moment = 0 and consider left section.750 x 5 - Ax * 1 - 1000 x 2.5 = 0.  ( Ax is the horizontal force at hinge support A). Ax = 1250 kg.

In a continuous bending moment curve the point where it changes sign, is called
	Deb, 2017, I think it should be only B.
	Vardhan, 2017, Yes, B is correct.
	Indu, 2017, Point of inflexion means where the shear force changes its sign, the point of a virtual hinge is about virtual work, the point of contra flexure means. Where the bending moment changes its sign. So, option B is correct.
	Kalyani, 2017, Yes, B is correct.
	Joydeep, 2018, @Indu.Point of inflexion means, that point where BM is 0 ( either BM changed sign or not ) In simply supported beam, always BM is 0 at support, this point is called a point of inflexion, & where BM changed sign only this point is called a point of contra flexture.We say that every point of contra flexure is the point of inflexion, but all point of inflexion does not point of contra flexure.
	Ayesha, 2019, Where BM changes its sign from +ve to -ve or -ve to +ve is called point of contraflexure and where deflection curve changes its nature from convex to concave or concave to convex is called point of inflection.What about point of virtual hinge? Can anybody explain?
	Jinish, 2019, Point of inflexion means:where BM change sign or concave to convex or point of the curve were downward to upward moment of curve and vice versa.Point of contra flexure: where BM is zero where bending moment curve intersects zero bending moment line.Point of virtual hinge: in continues beam, we have to provide construction etc type so it's an original joint together at the point where one can swing relative to the other.
	Kaku, 2021, Given answer is correct.



A beam of length L supported on two intermediate rollers carries a uniformly distributed load on its entire length. If sagging B.M. and hogging B.M. of the beam are equal, the length of each overhang, is
	Yousuf, 2016, Please explain clearly.
	Praveen, 2016, Please Explain the answer.
	Shakeel, 2017, How to solve it?
	Kajal Tomar, 2018, Max. Sagging B.M = w/8( L^2 -4a^2).Max hogging B.M = wa^2/2.Sagging B.M = Hogging B.M ( acc to ques);a = 0.207L.
	Naitik Trivedi, 2018, @Kajal.After solving this formula;Answer is l= 2a. a=0.5l (E) is correct answer.
	Nilraj, 2018, Kajal is right @Naitik.l=2a is a condition for zero bending moment at centre.
	Jinz, 2020, @Kajal how does it come out .207 L.
	Pravin, 2020, @All.Max. Sagging B.M = w/8( L^2 -4a^2).Max hogging B.M = wa^2/2.Sagging B.M = Hogging B.M, w/8( L^2 -4a^2)=wa^2/2, After solving;a=0.353l.
	Hamza Cheema, 2020, You are right, Thanks @Pravin.
	Prakash Basyal, 2021, Sagging B.M at the centre = Hogging B.M at support. -W*L/2*L/4+WL/2(L/2-X) = WX^2/2.L^2-4LX = 4X^2.L^2-4LX+4X^2 = 8X^2.(L-2X)^2 = 8X^2.X = 0.207L.  .So, B is correct.
	Sadulla, 2021, @Pravin.Yes, you are correct.

	Charles, 2015, Please show solution.

If all the dimensions of a bar are increased in the proportion n : 1, the proportion with which the maximum stress produced in the prismatic bar by its own weight, will increase in the ratio
	Ankit, 2016, Please explain it.
	Muhammad Zeeshan Khan, 2018, The formula of stress by its own weight is stress=wl/2AE if we increase by n:1 ie l by n and A by 1 than we get the which is increased by n:1.
	Deepak, 2018, I think the answer is D.
	Bharat, 2019, As stress = WL/2AE.If all dimensions increased in ration n:1 then length becomes n times original length and area becomes n^2 times original area and hence stress becomes 1/n times. So the answer is 1/n:1.
	Suraj, 2019, The answer is absolutely right, if you consider a section at  " x " distance from the bottom , weight of that portion W= density x volume = density  X area X length = d X A X x, for x= L, W= d X A X L, now stress = w/A, stress = d * L, so only L is variable here.
	Krish, 2019, Elongation due to self-weight, √L=WL/2AE.√L/L =W/2AE.Stress=(√L/L)*E= W/2A.Hence, in my opinion, the answer would be 1/n^2:1.
	Miller Jonson, 2020, It should be B.
	M K, 2020, Everyone is calculating Deflection but in the question they have asked the maximum stress not deflection. So, answer B is correct.
	Amit Kushwaha, 2020, Thanks @Suraj.
	Rot, 2020, Thank you for explaining @ M K.
	Rot, 2020, Thanks @Suraj.
	Dheeraj, 2021, Stress under own weight = wl^2/2E.Where w is unit weight,So, it might be 4 times.



The slenderness ratio of a vertical column of square cross- section of 10 cm side and 500 cm long, is
	Suhasini, 2015, Slenderness ratio = Length/Radius of gyration.Radius of gyration = SQRT (I/A).Moment of inertia (square) , I = b^4/12 = 10^4/12 = 833.33 cm4.Area of square column = 10*10 = 100 cm2.Radius of gyration = SQRT (833.33/10) = 2.8867 cm.Therefore Slenderness ratio, L/Radius of gyration = 500/2.8867 = 173.2.
	Naveed Kamal, 2017, S.r = L/r.r = sqrt I/A  ;( a x a3)/12 = a4/12 = ans ;a x a = ans ;a= 10 cm.r = ans ' L =500 cm. By applying this, you will get the answer.
	Shiva, 2017, Nice explanation @Suhasini.
	Amal Zad, 2017, Good, Thanks @Suhasini.
	Chauhan Hardik, 2018, The Radius of gyration = SQRT (833.33/10) = 2.8867 cm.how? because. 833.33/10 = 83.33.
	Shambhuu, 2018, @Chauhan.√(833.33/100) = 2.88.
	Vaibhav, 2019, @Shambu.How you got 833.33/100 = 2.88?
	Poj, 2020, @Vaibhav (SQRT) The square root of 833.33/10 = 2.8867.

	Reshu Agrawal, 2016, Can anyone give the solution of this question?

The cross sections of the beams of equal length are a circle and a square whose permissible bending stress are same under same maximum bending. The ratio of their flexural weights is,
	Ankit, 2016, How its possible?
	Asha, 2016, Anyone knows exact solution?
	Rahul Meena, 2017, Square/circle = 1/1.12.
	Naren, 2017, What is meant by flexural weight?
	Mohit, 2018, 0.894 is the approximate value.
	Usman Khan, 2018, Please anyone explain the solution.
	Kiran, 2019, Can anyone explain the solution?
	Devendra Singh, 2019, Stress the same relationship sectional modulus ratio. Circle sectional modulus= &pi& * d^3/16.sovle .19625.Square z = d^3/6. Solve 1/6=.1667.Ratio = .19625/.1667 = 1.118.
	Sunn, 2020, Could not understand it. Please explain in detail.
	Roshni, 2020, Flexural weight = density *volume.Density and length are the same, so the ratio of flexural weight comes to be the ratio of their area.Wcircle/Wsquare = ( (pi*d^2) /4) / a^2.Now, to find the ratio of d^2/a^2, we use the condition same bending stress.Bending stress=My/I, where M is the same for both, so this becomes section modulus of the circle equals section modulus of the square. And find the relation among d and a, substitute in the weight equation above the answer comes to be the option (A).
	Nishkarsha Dawadi, 2020, Since the density and length is same for two beams, the flexural weight now varies with their X-sectional area. So, we now have to find the ration of Ac/As.Also we have,M=Z*sigma.M and sigma are the same for two beams, so the section modulus Z also should be the same for two beams.i.e, Zcircle = Zsquare.or, Pi*r^3 /4 = b^3 /6.or, pi* {sq.root(Ac/pi)}^3 /4 = {sq.root (As)}^3 /6.after simplifying, we get;Ac/As = (2/3*pi^1/3)^2/3 = 1.11769.Which makes (A) the right answer.
	Vipin Sainath, 2020, Section Modulus of Circle/Square gives the ratio of flexural weight.Note: The C/S areas are same, So take Circle Diameter = 1.27m and Side of Sq = 1m, shall have the same area of cross-section   Zcircle = &P;i d^3/32.Zsquare = bd^2 / 6.Finally, Zcircle/Zsquare = 1.1189.
	Raj, 2021, @Devendra Singh The SECTION MODULUS FOR CIRCLE IS (π*d^3/32) polar sec mod is (π*d^3/16) so the correct answer is 0.589.

The areas of cross-section of a square beam and a circular beam subjected to equal bending moments, are same.
	Rajendra, 2015, Moment of inertia of square beam is more than that of circular beam by keeping same side/diameter. As M.I. increases, square beam is more economical.
	O.P Buswal, 2018, Nice explanation, thanks.
	Jayakrishnan, 2018, In rectangular beam, more particles are concentrated on extreme fibre than the circular beam. So they can take up maximum stress occuring at extreme fibres.
	Lalit Sengar, 2019, M.I. of the square beam is more than the circular beam, therefore, bending stress is lower in the square for the same cross-section area. You can find the dia of the circular section by equating both areas. Lower the bending stress better than the cross-section.
	Ashok, 2019, Thanks for giving more clarity.
	Komal, 2021, Bending stress is more at top and bottom fibres, so there should be more materials needed. And in circular section less material at top and bottom as compared to rectangular section so it is safe against bending stress and economical too.Circular section is good in shear as compared to rectangular section because shear stress is more at centre and lower at top and bottom fibre.

If the atmospheric pressure on the surface of an oil tank (sp. gr. 0.8) is 0.1 kg/cm2, the pressure at a depth of 2.5 m, is
	Kanishk, 2013, How come the answer is C?Initial Pressure in terms of ht. of water column= 1000/(1000*10)=0.1 m --- (i).Pressure @ 2.5 m depth in oil tank = 2.5*800*10.Representing above pressure as ht of water column, the answer comes out to be 2.0 m --- (ii).(i) + (ii) = 2.1m.
	Nikku, 2013, Pressure head in terms of water = 2.5/0.8 = 3.1m.
	Hitesh Kumar, 2013, Total pressure = ((.1*10^4)/1000)+(.8*2.5) = 3.
	Kumar, 2014, Initial pressure is 1000 kg/m^2.Pressure at 2.5 m depth is 19620 kg/m^2.Total pressure at a depth 2.5 m is = 19620+1000 = 20620 kg/m^2.Depth in terms of water = 20620/(1000*9.81) = 2.1 m.
	Ruben, 2015, From Basic Hydro static principle (FM text book by Frank m White).P = Pa-Gamma (Z2-Z1).Where Pa = Atmospheric pressure in n/m2 Z1 = 0 (Reference surface) and Z2 = negative downwards.= (0.98 n/m2-7848(-2.5)).= 19620.8 pa.H = (19620.8/(1000*9.81)) = 2 m of water.
	Nikita Patil, 2015, How do you calculate the pressure at the given height? Please explain me.
	Manpraveen, 2015, P = &dollar;gh.Pressure for oil = 0.8*1000*9.81*2.5 = 19620 n/m^2.Pressure for water = 1000*9.81*h = 19620.H1 = 19620/9810 = 2 m.H2 = atmospheric head = p/&dollar;g.H2 = 1000*9.81/1000*9.81= 1 m.Total head = H1+H2 = 2+1 = 3 m (Answer).
	Ma Murtaza, 2015, p*ATM = 0.1 kg/cm*2 = 1000 kg/m*2 = 9810 N/m*2.p*oil = 19620 N/m*2 (after calculation).Total pressure = 29430 N/m*2.Hence, h*water = 29430/9810 = 3 m of water.
	Abdilahi Khalif, 2015, 1000/(1000+2.5*0.8) = 3.
	Rizgar Karim, 2015, Patm = 0.1kg/cm2 = 0.1*10000*9.81/1000 = 9.81 KN/m2.Poil = 0.8*9.81*2.5 = 19.62 KN/m2.Ptotal = Patm + Poil.Ptotal = 19.62 + 9.81 = 29.43 KN/m2.P in-term of water = 29.43/9.81 = 3m of water.
	Ccjayesh, 2016, Pa = 0.1 kg/cm2 = 1000 kg/m2.Poil = 800*2.5 = 2000 kg/m2.Total = 3000 kg/m2 = 3000/1000 = 3 meters of water.
	Anonymous, 2016, P2 = P1 + Density(H); P1 = 0.1kg/cm^2 or 1000kg/m2.Density of oil = specific Gravity x 1000 kg/m3. = 800kg/m3 (Note: 1000kg/m3 is the density of water).P2 = 1000 + 800 x 2.5 = 3000kg/m2.Pressure in H of Water = 3000/1000 = 3.0m.
	Kailash Paliwal, 2016, Atmosphere pressure =.1kg/cm2 = 1000 kg/m2.1000kg/1000kg = 1m of water.Oil pressure = 2.5m = 2.5 *.8 = 2m of water. Total height = 1 + 2 = 3 m. Hence c is right.
	Bahram, 2016, First Patm = 0.1kg/sq/cm = 1000 kg/sq.m.Now Poil = S(oil) * ρ(water) *h(oil) => 0.8 * 1000 * 2.5 = 2,000 kg/sq.m.So, P(total at bottom) = 2,000 + 1,000 = 3,000 kg/sq.m.Now h(pressure in terms of head of water) = P(total)/ρ(water) =>  3,000 kg/sq.m/1,000 kg/cu.m = 3 m of water.
	Komala Sai, 2016, Patm = 0.1kg/cm^2=> 1000kg/m^2.Poil = (0.8 * 1000) * 2.5= 2000 kg/m^2.Finally in terms of water = (1000 + 2000) &div; 1000,= 3m of water.
	Rajani Patel, 2017, Pressure at surface =10000kg/m^2.Pressure at a depth of 2.5 m=(2.5*9.81*800)=19620.Total pressure=29620.H=P/1000*9.81=29620/1000*9.81.H = 3.01m of water.

	Sashaus, 2017, S1 h1 = s2 h2 or  £1h1 = £2h2. £ = density , s= sp.gr.1000 h1 = 800 * 2.5. H1 = 2 + 1 (initially pressure is 1) = 3.

	Missaram Choudhary, 2013, Density of liquid = m/v = 3000/4 = 750kg/m3Density of liquid/density of water = 750/1000 = 0.75 = specific gravity.

A syphon is used
	Shoaib Mazarzai Baloch, 2015, It is used in different points intervened by a hill.
	Shivam Kr Gupta, 2017, A long bend pipe, used to carry water from a reservoir at a higher level to another reservoir at a lower level when two reservoirs are separated by a hill, is called as syphon. The highest point of syphon is called as summit.The highest point of syphon lies above the free water surface in the reservoir at higher level. Therefore, pressure at this point is below atmospheric. The negative or vacuum pressure is created in the syphon, so that liquid gets pushed into it.Syphon works on Bernoulli's principle and it is used when the water have to take out from reservoir to another separated by a hill. Syphon is also used to drain out water from a channel without any outlet.
	S.H.Patil, 2018, Nice explanation. Thanks.
	Sunil, 2018, Nice explanation Thank you @Shivam.
	Lakhan Meena, 2019, Thank you @Shivam.
	Ercivilian, 2019, Thanks @Shivam.
	Sarath S, 2020, A long bent pipe connecting two reservoirs whose upper curvature lies above the hydraulic gradient. A syphon is installed in a way such that no section of pipe lies more than 7.6m above the hydraulic gradient line.
	Soumya, 2020, Thank you all for the explanation.
	Eshetie, 2020, Very nice explanation, thanks @Shivam.
	Prashant, 2020, Why not option C?

Unit of kinematic viscosity is
	Nidhi Hinal, 2013, Unit of dynamic viscosity = Ns/m2.Unit of density = kg/m3.Now, kinetic viscosity = dynamic viscosity/density.                              = (N sec/m2)/(kg/m3).                       = (mass x gravity x sec/m2)/(kg/m3).                       = (kg x m/sec2) x (sec/m2)/(kg/m3).                       = m2/sec.
	Utpal, 2016, Thank you, @Nidhi Hinal.
	Raja, 2016, Nice explanation @Nidhi Hinal.
	Sachin, 2016, Nice explanation @Nidhi Hinal.
	Era Examiner, 2016, Thank you @Nidhi.
	Parashuram Kagi, 2016, kinametic viscocity k = dynamic viscocity/density.Dynamic viscocity = N-sec/m^2.Density = m^3/sec.                            Hence K= N-sec/m^3/m^3/sec.k = N/m^2.
	Mahesh Bisen, 2016, Good explanation, thanks @ @Nidhi Hinal.
	Rajkumar, 2018, Excellent explanations. Thanks All.
	Abhisek, 2019, Kinametic viscocity k = dynamic viscocity/density.Dynamic viscocity = N-sec/m^2.Density = kg/m^3.Hence K = (N-sec/m^3)/(kg/m^3).1N = Kg-m/s^2.So K = ((Kg-m/s^2)/m^3)/(kg/m^3).K = m^2/s.



The moment of inertia of a floating body along its longitudinal axis and the volume of water displaced by it are I and V respectively. The height of the metacentre above centre of buoyancy of the body, is
	Rahul, 2017, Metacentric height = I/V-BG, so the height of metacenter above B will be I/V.
	Bhanu, 2017, BM=MOI(min)/volume(displaced),So that                                                                                                                                 The height of metacenter will be I/V.
	Priyanka Shah, 2019, Please explain the answer in clear.
	Karthi, 2019, What is below the centre of the body?
	Jinish, 2019, If the metacentre is above the centre of gravity, buoyancy restores stability when the ship tilts. The stability increases with the distance between metacentre and centre of gravity called the metacentric height. If the metacentre is below the centre of gravity, the boat is unstable, and a tilt results in capsizing.

Critical depth (h) of a channel, is
	Javed, 2015, As it depends on froudes number. For critical flow froudes number is one and the depth corresponding to critical flow is called critical depth.Froudes number = Velocity/Root of height*velocity.
	Divakar, 2016, What's v2/g? Please explain.
	Vishu, 2016, It is possible only for the rectangular channel.
	Sreesha, 2016, As we know, that v is directly proportional to √ g*h according to Froude's law.
	Ravi, 2016, Froud number equals to 1 for critical so (V/(√gh)):1.
	Bapugouda, 2016, Please, anyone give me the explanation of the answer.
	Abhishek Anand, 2017, The depth of water in a channel when vel of flow is critical or when the specific energy is min is called critical depth of the channel.Critical vel of flow is the vel at which sp.energy is minimum.Specific energy ->    E=h+(v^2/2g).For minimization,   dE/dh =0.On putting v=Q/A.We get ,  1=v^2/gh.So, V= (gh)^1/2 or sqrt of gh.Which is critical velocity;So, critical depth= v^2/g.
	Vaibhav Patle, 2017, No, the right answer is v2/2g.
	Sandip Buktare, 2017, For critical depth = v/root of height * gravity.1 = v/root of height * gravity,The root of height * gravity = v,Taking square of both side. h * g = v^2h = v^/g.
	Santhosh C, 2017, V in the above equation is critical velocity. That is velocity with respect to the critical depth of flow.
	Santhosh C, 2017, Critical velocity Vc = root of g * hc(Critical depth of flow).
	Rohit, 2017, V^2/2g is the right answer.
	Nitesh Saini, 2017, A is correct answer. Reason behind that v^2/2g is a term used in specific energy not for critical depth i.e. E= h+v^2/2g = specific energy.But critical depth is that depth at which specific energy is minimum so we have to differentiate above equation w.r.t h ... And then term comes dE/dh= 1+ q^2/2g(-2/h^3)=0 ... And simplify this equating we will get h= q^2/g .... i.e. (av)^2/g... i.e. v^2/g.I hope you will understand what i m saying good luck friends for your future.
	Kajal Ncp, 2018, Thanks @Nitesh.
	Thundir, 2018, E = y + v^2/2g is specific energy.Here, v^2/2g is KINETIC energy and "y" is POTENTIAL energy.So, the given answer is correct.
	Anurag, 2019, I think [q^2/g]^[1/3].
	Amare Yihunie, 2019, For critical flow type specific energy = yc + v2/2g.for rectangular channel = Ec = 1.5 yc.1.5 yc = yc + v2/2g,0.5 yc = v2/2g,yc = v2/g.
	Priyanka Shah, 2019, Thank you @Nitesh.
	Prassy, 2020, hc=( q2/g)^1/3.Taking cube on both sides hc^3= q2/g.q=Q/b= A*V/b= (b*h*V)/b= (h*v),hc^3= (hc * V)^2/g,hc= V2/g.

The shear stress distribution in viscous fluid through a circular pipe is :
	Kanishk, 2013, The expression is given as:Tau = del(p)/del(x) * r/2.where r = distance from the central axis.So the answer should be B.
	Ujwal, 2013, Shear stress is maximum at the pipe faces and will be minimum at the center. Velocity will be max at the center and minimum at the face of pipe.
	Magnus Hwosafe, 2013, The answer is correct; the velocity is maximum @ the centre hence the shear stress being proportional to the velocity gradient is also maximum.
	Prashant Tomar, 2015, Shear stress is minimum in center and maximum at face.
	Ejaz Ahmad, 2015, In circular pipe Shear stress is minimum at center and maximum at face inside the pipe because it varies with are i.e radius keeping dp/dx is constant but in case of velocity i.e velocity is maximum at center and minimum at face inside the pipe.Hence answer should be B.

A cylinder 3 m in diameter and 4 m long retains water one side as shown in the below figure. If the weight of the cylinder is 2000 kgf, the horizontal reaction at B is 
	Ranjith, 2015, Can you explain?
	Sushant, 2015, Can anybody explain how it comes?
	Ratan, 2015, Horizontal force F = whA.w = 1000*g.A = 3*4 = 12 m2.h = 3/2.F = 1000*12*1.5*g = 18000kgf.
	Rizgar Karim, 2015, Specific weight of water = 9.81 KN/m3.Horizontal area = 3*4 = 12 m2.Depth of center of gravity from the water surface = 3/2 = 1.5 m.Horizontal force = Reaction at B = 9.81*12*1.5 = 176.58 KN/m2.= 176.58*1000 = 176580 N/m2.= 176580/9.81 = 18000 Kgf.
	Sagar Radadiya, 2017, Pressure at middle of pipe = pgh.= 1000*9.81*1.5 (pressure is min. at depth 0 & max. at depth 3m, so average 1.5m is taken). = 14715 kg/sq. mt.So, Force at B  = Pressure * Area.                        = 14715*(3*4),                        = 176580 N,                        = 176580/9.81 kgf,                        = 18000 kgf.
	Samynathan, 2017, How horizontal area is 3*4?
	Arshad Nirwan, 2017, 3 meter (dia of pipe) also known as  (hight),4 meter long (size of pipe) also known as  (length),So, horizontal area is = height * length  (3 * 4)=12.

While applying the Bernoulli's equation  = total head, the work any section done on the flow system, if any
	Magnus Hwosafe, 2013, I think the answer to this question is wrong, because, Bernoulli's equation represents conservation of energy. When a fluid does work, it is usually accompanied with some loss of energy which should be added to the right side of the equation.
	Dhiraj Bhise, 2015, Yes, I am also agree with you @Magnus Hwosafe.
	Rahul, 2015, It is added to the left side of the equation because work done is to increase the head, and it is subtracted if it has losses.
	Ramavtar, 2017, @Rahul,Work done is output plus and head loss minus.
	Rahul Gupta, 2018, Correct answer is A it is added in the right side of the equation, because it is based on the conservation of energy.
	Pawan, 2018, I think the Answer is right because work is done on 'any section' needed energy so it (lost energy) must be added to the 'any section's side. It does not depend upon it is written on right side or left.
	Nilraj, 2018, @All.In question- work done on flow system means increases energy which should be added to the left side.If work done by the flow means a decrease in energy which should be added to the right side.So the answer is correct.
	Jeevan, 2019, Well said @Nilraj.

Hydrostatic pressure on a dam depends upon its
	Krishna Chaitanya, 2015, Because Hydrostatic pressure increases with Depth from Hydrostatic Law and also depends on shape of the dam.

	Anomie, 2019, You can simply differentiate the hydrostatic force resistance by various shapes of dam as under:1. Gravity dam:- By self weight.2. Arch dam:- By arch action.3. Lock Gates:- By hinges and sides.4. Cylindrical water tank (Not considered in dam, but just for reference): Strength of cylindrical shell.

	Saifu, 2019, P = pgh...It depends upon depth only.
	Jinish Patel, 2019, It depends upon depth because pgh formula of hydrostatic pressure and dam is constructed with different shape like gravity dam (resist by self weight) and arch dam (resist by arch action) and concrete dam etc and function of them different as per shape and when we consider the material of the dam are always different for small dam gravity and arch dam whenever big dams materials have been mass concrete like Gama material. And well said anome different water tanks have been made of different materials as concrete and thermoplastic depends also on their shape underground tank, the overhead tank also have different shapes. So it's depends upon depth, shape, and material also.
	Akv, 2019, Hydrostatic pressure at any point in a liquid depends only on the depth and density of a liquid. But hydrostatic pressure 'on a dam' is the sum of pressure by liquid acting on the surface in contact with liquid within the dam. So it depends on the depth as well as the shape of a dam.
	Shankar Shaud, 2021, If it is pressure intensity, it depends on depth only. If it is pressure force it depends on depth, shape and length. I think the question should be hydrostatic pressure per unit length.So, the given answer is correct.
	Suresh, 2021, @PeshalHere, h depends upon shape so every shape has a different value of h. So pressure varies with shape also as for concerned to dams.

A pipe of 0.1 m2 cross sectional area suddenly enlarges to 0.3 m2 cross-sectional area. If the discharge of the pipe is 0.3 m3 /sec, the head loss is
	Taba Tallum, 2014, h(en) = ( v1-v2)^2 / 2g.     = (Q/A1 - Q/A2)^2 / 2g.     = (0.3/0.1 - 0.3/0.3)^2 / 2g.     = (3-1)^2 / 2g.     = 2/g m of water.
	Abhijit, 2016, Nice explanation @Taba Tallum.
	Sai, 2016, Thanks @Taba Tallum.
	Dar, 2016, Thanks @Taba Tallum.

	Sakthimunivel, 2017, Thanks for the explanation @Taba.
	Dineah Prasad Sao, 2017, V1 = .3/.1 = 3V2 = .3/.3 = 1Head loss = v^2/2g.                  = (3-1)^2/2g,                   = 2/g.
	Hitesh, 2017, Good, Thanks @Dineah.
	Kiccha, 2017, Thanks for explaining it.
	Vinod, 2017, Thanks for the given solution.
	Shree, 2018, Thank you @Taba Tallum.
	Rajesh, 2018, Thank you somuch.
	Amare Yihunie, 2018, Q1 = A1 * V1,V1 = Q1/A1,Q = 0.3,A1 = 0.1,V = 0.3/0.1 = 3.Q2 = Q1 = 0.3.= A2 * V2, V2 = Q2/A2, 0.3/0.1=1.HL = (V1-V2)^2/2g,(3-1)^2/2g.=2^2/2g,4/2g,=2/g.

Maximum efficiency of transmission of power through a pipe, is
	Md Rafi, 2016, Efficiency = (H-hf)/H.For max. Power transmission through pipe the condition is hf = H/3.Substituting the value of hf in efficiency, we get max Efficiency.Therefore, max. Effi. = [(H-H/3)/H] =1 " (1/3) = 2/3 = 66.67%.

Reynold number is the ratio of initial force and
	Rishi, 2016, Reynold number = I.F/V.F.Froude number = (I.F/G.F)^0.5.Mach number = (I.F/E.F)^0.5.Euler number = (I.F/P.F)^0.5.Weber number = (I.F/S.T)^0.5.Where,I.F Inertia force.V.F Viscous force.G.F Gravity force.E.F Elastic force.P.F Pressure force.W.F Surface tension.

	Anuj, 2018, Good @Rishi.

	Ercivilian, 2019, Thanks @Rishi.
	Jinish Patel, 2019, Reynolds no. = Inertia force/ viscosity = pH/u. u is viscosity.

If D is the diameter of a pipe of length L and f is the coefficient of friction of pipe then diameter of the nozzle d is
	Prince, 2018, Anybody please explain it.
	Khushwant Rana, 2018, For maximum transmission, H=3h,where H=Total Supply Head,h=head lost due to friction,H=3h.=>v^2/2g=3 flV^2/(2gD),where, v=velocity at the nozzle end,V=velocity in pipe.=>Q^2/(a^2.2g)=3flQ^2/(A^2/2gD).where a=nozzle area,A=area of pipe,=>3fld^4=D^5.=>d= (D^5/(3fl))^(1/4).

	Brijesh Patel, 2017, For a curved surface, the component of hydrostatic force in any horizontal direction is equal to the hydrostatic force on the projected plane surface on a vertical plane perpendicular to that direction and acts through the centre of pressure for the projected plane area. The vertical component of hydrostatic force on a submerged curved surface is equal to the weight of the liquid volume vertically above the submerged surface to the level of the free surface of liquid and acts through the centre of gravity of the liquid in that volume.

A tank 4m x 3m x 2m containing an oil of specific gravity 0.83 is moved with an acceleration g/2 m sec2. The ratio of the pressures at its bottom when it is moving vertically up and down, is
	Umesh, 2016, How please explain?
	Sak, 2016, How please explain?
	Andhokar, 2016, Please explain the solution.
	Alo, 2016, Pressure ,P = Rho*g*h.According to the condition.When it is moving vertically up, g = ( 9.8 - 9.8/2).when it is moving vertically down, g = ( 9.8 + 9.8/2).Then Ratio = .83*( 9.8+9.8/2)*2 : .83*( 9.8-9.8/2)*2.So it must me 3 : 1.
	Aks, 2016, Good job @Alo.
	R K Budumuru, 2016, Ratio of the pressures moving vertically up and down should be 1 : 3 not 3 : 1.
	Abhishek Anand, 2017, Effective g = g+a for upward motion. = g-a for downward motion.So ans = 3 : 1.
	Dhanu, 2017, Correct @Abhishek.
	Garry, 2018, The given answer is correct. It will be 3 by 1.When the body will go upward acceleration will be G+a and when the body will go downward acceleration will be G-a.
	Monu, 2018, Why height is taken same for both condition? @Alo.
	Shama, 2018, @Monu. Height will be same moving from the top of the tank to bottom of the tank and vice versa i.e 2m.
	Vivek Bahra, 2018, For upward acc = (p-pa)= wh(1+a/g).For downward acc = (p-pa)= wh(1-a/g).Substituting the value of a = g/2, the ratio of this comes out to be 3:1.

The ratio of the inertia and gravitational force acting in any flow, ignoring other forces, is called
	Anurag Labh, 2015, Frode number is ratio of inertia and gravitational force, Euler number is the ratio of inertia and pressure force, weber number is the ratio of inertia and surface tension, Reynolds number is the ratio of inertia and viscous force.
	Anil, 2016, What is the value of each?
	Adeel Rehman, 2020, Reynold number = I.F/V.F.Froude number = (I.F/G.F)^0.5.Mach number = (I.F/E.F)^0.5.Euler number = (I.F/P.F)^0.5.Weber number = (I.F/S.T)^0.5.Where,I.F Inertia force.V.F Viscous force.G.F Gravity force.E.F Elastic force.P.F Pressure force.S.T Surface tension.

Hydraulic ram is a device
	Mohit, 2018, A hydraulic ram, or hydram, is a cyclic water pump powered by hydropower. The device uses the water hammer effect to develop pressure that allows a portion of the input water that powers the pump to be lifted to a point higher than where the water originally started.

Water displaced by a floating wooden block of density 0.75, 5 m long, 2 m wide and 3 m high, is
	Deep, 2014, Force = Density*Area*Height.F = 0.75*2*3*5 = 22.5.

	Suryaaa, 2020, Maybe 0.75 is specific gravity.
	Cm Nayak, 2020, The volume of wood = 5*3*2=30 cum.Weight of wood = vol *density of the wood. The density of wood= SG*density of water.W = 30*0.75*1000 = 22500 kg.weight of wood = weight of water displaced.Therefore vol.of water displaced = weight of water displaced/density.i.e V=22.5 cum.



In pipe lines, a surge tank is provided
	Ravi Chandra, 2016, This is  shown in the impulse turbine when the penstock after surge will provide the surge can control the pressure in penstock indirectly its control the water hammering effect also.
	Pari, 2019, Surge tank is a water storage device used as pressure neutralizer in hydropower water conveyance system to resists excess pressure rise and pressure drop conditions. A surge tank (or surge drum or surge pool) is a standpipe or storage reservoir at the downstream end of a closed aqueduct, feeder, dam, barrage pipe to absorb sudden rises of pressure, as well as to quickly provide extra water during a brief drop in pressure.

Flow in pipes is laminar if Reynold number is
	Bhaktidesh, 2015, In pipe flow is laminar if R is less than 2000.
	Siva, 2016, In Laminar, the Flow is less than 2000.
	Sahana, 2017, Re no for laminar flow is less than 2000.Re no for turbulent flow is more than 2000.If Re lies B/w 500-2000 it is transition state flow.
	Megha Manju, 2018, It's laminar if it's less than 2100.turbulent if it is more than 4000.and transitional flow if it's between 2100 and 4000.
	Sagar, 2018, It's laminar if it's less than 2100.It's turbulent if more than 2800.And between 2100 to 2800 neither turbulent nor laminar.
	Imran.Arshad223@Gmail.Com, 2019, Which one is accurate?
	Bipin, 2021, It should be less than 2000.
	Vinoto, 2021, Less than 2100 - Laminar. 2100 - 4000 - Transition.More than 4000 - Turbulent.
	Vinoto, 2021, Less than 2100 - Laminar. 2100 - 4000 - Transition.More than 4000 - Turbulent.
	Ching, 2021, Flow with Reynolds number less than 2000 is considered as a laminar flow while flow with Reynolds number greater than 4000 is considered as turbulent flow. Flow having Reynolds number between 2000 to 4000 known as transition flow.

	Lalit Pathade, 2019, Euler's Equation is based on some assumptions: 1. Non-viscous flow. 2. Incompressible  and homogeneous flow. 3. Steady flow. 4. Continuous flow. 5. Uniform velocity of the flow.

Mach number is the ratio of inertia force to
	Piyush, 2017, Inertia force to elastic force is Cauchy no.
	Sri, 2018, Ratio of inertia force to elasticity is Cauchy number.Ratio of flow velocity past a boundary to local speed of sound is Mach number.
	Niraj, 2018, Square root of ratio of inertia force to elastic force is mach number.
	Abubakar, 2020, The ratio of the speed of a body to the speed of sound in the surrounding medium. It is often used with a numeral (as Mach 1, Mach 2, etc.) to indicate the speed of sound, twice the speed of sound, etc.
	Akhil Sharma, 2020, Reynold number = I.F/V.F.Froude number = (I.F/G.F)^0.5.Mach number = (I.F/E.F)^0.5.Euler number = (I.F/P.F)^0.5.Weber number = (I.F/S.T)^0.5.Where,I.F Inertia force.V.F Viscous force.G.F Gravity force.E.F Elastic force.P.F Pressure force.S.T Surface tension.

For critical depth of flow of water in open channels, fc the specific energy must be :
	Nyan, 2017, Discharge is maximum.
	Nazim Hussain, 2021, At critical depth, the specific energy is minimum and discharge is maximum.
	Khattak Khan, 2021, The characteristics of critical flow are:(i) The specific energy and specific force is minimum for the given discharge.(ii) The Froude number is equal to unity.(iii) For given specific energy the discharge is maximum at the critical flow.(iv) The velocity head is equal to half the hydraulic depth in a channel of a small slope.(v) Flow at the critical state is unstable.

A jet projected at an angle of 45θ, 40 m from the foot of a vertical column, just reaches the top of the column. The height of the column is
	Chandra Mohan Mahato, 2015, Height of column should be 20 mtr.
	Jwala, 2016, Answer 10 m.
	Aditya Ch, 2016, Answer is correct. Applying Pythagoras theorem, tan 45 = x/40, therefore x = 40 * 1 = 40.
	Krishan Suthar, 2017, Yes, Correct @Aditya.
	Krishan Suthar, 2017, Yes, Correct @Aditya.
	Garry, 2018, The path of the particle will be a parabolic so tan θ will not be applied the correct answer from the projectile motion will come to be 20 m.And I also agree @Mahato.
	Jony, 2018, Please derive the expression @Garry.
	Priya, 2018, Horizontal range  40*2=u^2sin2θ/g. We get u=28.Hmax=u^2sin(θ)^2/2g Hmax=20m.
	Nikhil, 2018, 40tan45°= 40m.
	Viplav, 2018, 40=v^2sin(90)/g.V=20.H=20^2 sin(45)^2/2g=15.Answer is 15.
	Gege, 2018, From any point jet has departed 45° and reaches the height of column (H).The distance between from any point to the tip of the column is 40m.This can be calculated using " Angle of Elevation"Tan(45°) = H/40; H = 40Tan(45°).H = 40m.
	Anjaligangwar, 2020, tan45° = height of the column/distance between jet and column (By Pythagoras theorem).
	Pkota, 2021, Projectile motion.Range R=u^2sin2θ/g.Height H=u^2(sinθ)^2/2g,Here 40m is R/2 so R=80,i.e.R=u^2sin2(45°)/g ; 80=u^2/g.H = u^2 (sin45°)^2/2g =40* (1/√2)^2=20m.

Atmospheric pressure varies with
	Vilas Tembhurne, 2013, Atmospheric pressure varies with the altitude.
	Kashi Ram Upadhayaya, 2015, Do you have any reason?
	Maulik Joshi, 2016, Altitude increases the atmospheric pressure decreases.
	Shuaan, 2016, Increases with Altitude. Atmospheric Pressure has nothing to do with weather condition or temperature.
	Pawan, 2016, I think d is the right answer. Atmospheric pressure at any point is numerically equal to the weight of the air column of the unit x sectional area. Temperature can vary the weight of the air. Weather conditions also vary the weight of air.
	Manish, 2016, The correct answer is option A.
	Ehsan Khosa, 2017, I think Option A is the correct answer.
	Prasun, 2017, A is the answer.At an elevation of 10 km (6 miles or about 30,000 feet), roughly the height of Mt. Everest, pressure drops to 265 millibars. That's less than 30% of the pressure at sea level! Both atmospheric pressure and the density of air decrease with altitude.
	Rinku Meena, 2017, This happens when the temperature of air is increased. Warm air molecules have more energy, so they move faster and create more pressure. Likewise, cold air has less energy and therefore exerts less pressure on its surroundings. The denser the air is, the more molecules there are in that given space.
	Saeed, 2018, I think A is the correct answer.
	Mukul, 2019, Altitude is the correct answer.
	Abhik, 2019, Warm air are more faster and create more pressure. So it depends.
